info.parenttitle: 3.2 Variations in economic activity - aggregate demand and aggregate supply

markdown:
1. The **Price Level** (**PL**) of an economy** is not the only** **factor** that **affects** its **short-run aggregate supply** (**SRAS**). 
2. While changes in the **PL** cause **movements** along the **SRAS curve**, changes in the **determinants of short-run aggregate supply** **shift** the **SRAS curve**.

<definition term="Determinants of short-run aggregate supply">Factors that **shift** the **SRAS** curve.</definition>

The **determinants of SRAS** covered in the IB curriculum are:

1. **Costs of factors of production**.
2. **Indirect taxes**.

<callout type="note">The most important thing to note in this topic is that:

1. Changes in **price level** cause changes the quantity of **real GDP** produced   (causing a **movement** along the **SRAS curve**).
2. Changes in the **determinants of SRAS **lead to changes in the quantity of **real GDP** produced at **all possible price levels** (causing a **shift **of the **SRAS curve**).
  1. A **rightward shift** indicates that at all given price levels, the total quantity of **real GDP produced** **increases**.
  2. A **leftward shift** indicates that at all given price levels, the total quantity of **real GDP produced decreases**.

![Figure 1: movements versus shifts of the short-run aggregate supply curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ef64e1553451aeeb7c705e6059bf1803f5553add-2398x1014.png)</callout>

<callout type="exam_technique">In IB Economics long-answer questions, it is very important to explain processes step-by-step, without skipping any steps.

In this section, the effect of changes in the determinants of SRAS will be explained in such way.</callout>

## Costs of factors of production


#### Increase in the costs of factors of production → leftward shift in SRAS


1. **Factors of production** refer to the **resources** **used** in the **production process (**land, labour, capital, entrepreneurship).
2. If the **costs** of these factors **increase** (higher wages, rising raw material prices...), **production** becomes **more** **expensive** for firms.
3. As a result, to **maintain** **profit** **margins**, firms across the economy **decrease** their **production** at **all possible price levels**.
4. Since **short-run aggregate supply (SRAS)** reflects the **total** **output** (real GDP) firms produce **at different price levels**...
5. An **increase** in **production costs** **reduces** **SRAS **at** all price levels**, and shifts the **SRAS curve** to the **left** (SRAS1→SRAS2).

![Figure 2: leftward shift of the SRAS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/0c108991043e3d1628788360a14e86075af80b3c-2400x1056.png)

#### Decrease in the costs of factors of production → rightward shift in SRAS


1. **Factors of production** refer to the **resources** **used** in the **production process (**land, labour, capital, entrepreneurship).
2. If the **costs** of these factors **decrease** (lower wages, falling raw material prices...), **production** becomes **cheaper** for firms.
3. As a result, seeking **greater** **profit** **margins**, firms across the economy **increase** their **production** at **all possible price levels**.
4. Since **short-run aggregate supply (SRAS)** reflects the **total** **output** (real GDP) firms produce **at different price levels**...
5. A **decrease** in **production costs** **increases** **SRAS **at** all price levels**, and shifts the **SRAS curve** to the **right** (SRAS1→SRAS2).

![Figure 3: rightward shift of the SRAS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/9e047e533a38e8797b2ada917b718ec75e827b33-2400x1056.png)

## Indirect taxes


<definition term="Indirect taxes">**Taxes** levied on **spending on goods and services.** They are called **indirect** because while consumers contribute to part or all of the tax, it is the **suppliers (firms)** who **collect** and **transfer** these **taxes** to the **government authorities **(consumers pay the taxes **indirectly**).</definition>

#### Increase in indirect taxes → leftward shift in SRAS


1. When **indirect** **taxes** **increase**, firms face **higher** **costs** **for** **producing** goods and services, as they are **responsible** for **paying** **these** **taxes** to the government.
2. Facing **higher** **production** **costs**, in order to **maintain profit margins**, firms across the economy **decrease** their **production** at **all possible price levels**.
3. Since **short-run aggregate supply (SRAS)** reflects the **total** **output** (real GDP) firms produce **at different price levels**...
4. An **increase** in **indirect taxes** **decreases** **SRAS **at** all price levels**, and shifts the **SRAS curve** to the **left** (SRAS1→SRAS2).

![Figure 4: leftward shift of the SRAS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/0c108991043e3d1628788360a14e86075af80b3c-2400x1056.png)

#### Decrease in indirect taxes → rightward shift in SRAS


1. When **indirect** **taxes** **decrease**, firms face **lower** **costs** **for** **producing** goods and services, as they are **responsible** for **paying** **these** **taxes** to the government.
2. Facing **lower** **production** **costs**, seeking **greater profit margins**, firms across the economy **increase** their **production** at **all possible price levels**.
3. Since **short-run aggregate supply (SRAS)** reflects the **total** **output** (real GDP) firms produce **at different price levels**...
4. A **decrease** in **indirect taxes** **increases** **SRAS **at** all price levels**, and shifts the **SRAS curve** to the **right** (SRAS1→SRAS2).

![Figure 5: rightward shift of the SRAS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/9e047e533a38e8797b2ada917b718ec75e827b33-2400x1056.png)

<callout type="self_review">Go over all the **determinants of SRAS** and try to explain their effect on the **SRAS** and the **SRAS curve** step-by-step.

If you master this, Paper 1 will become much easier!</callout>
